channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Informatics] The Benefits Of Cognitive Inclusion In UX Research
item.description:
Documents a small but useful UX research study where participants with cognitive disabilities surfaced substantially more usability issues and suggestions than general-population participants, especially around content, affordances, visual elements, media, predictability, and cognitive load.

  • The study found 197 issues from cognitive participants versus 113 from general-population participants, and 93 suggestions versus 54.
  • Cognitive participants should be included in ordinary product research, not only accessibility audits.
  • Task completion is too thin a metric; ask about energy, focus, predictability, distractions, and confidence.
  • The sample is small and partly AI-prototyped, so treat the numbers as directional evidence and the method as the durable lesson.

item.title: [Informatics] Creating Memorable Web Experiences: A Modern CSS Toolkit
item.description:
Shows how current CSS and SVG features can carry expressive interaction without defaulting to JavaScript-heavy animation, while keeping motion tied to intent, accessibility, and browser-native performance.

  • Define the intended emotional and interaction vocabulary before adding motion; animation without intent adds noise.
  • Modern CSS primitives such as scroll-driven animations, view transitions, custom properties, masks, clip paths, and 3D transforms can replace heavier interaction stacks.
  • Split-text and SVG animation techniques need explicit accessibility handling because visual text manipulation can confuse assistive technology.
  • Choose clipping for hard geometric cuts and masks for soft edges, gradients, textures, or complex shapes.

item.title: [Informatics] Scroll-Driven, Scroll-Triggered, Scroll States, and View Transitions
item.description:
Clarifies four browser-platform concepts that are easy to conflate: scroll-driven animation, scroll-triggered animation, container query scroll state, and view transitions.

  • Scroll-driven animation ties animation progress directly to scroll progress.
  • Scroll-triggered animation starts when an element crosses a threshold, then runs independently of scroll progress.
  • Container query scroll state updates styles when a scroll condition such as sticky position is met.
  • View transitions are a separate API for same-document and cross-document visual transitions, not a scroll feature.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Dependency models in npm, Yarn, pnpm, Bun, and Deno
item.description:
Compares JavaScript package managers by dependency model rather than speed: npm favors compatibility, Yarn Berry favors reproducibility, pnpm favors explicit dependency boundaries, Bun favors fast integrated tooling, and Deno favors explicit web-native security.

  • Package-manager migrations fail when tools assume a different filesystem and module-resolution model, even if app code and lockfiles look correct.
  • npm hides undeclared dependency access through hoisting; pnpm exposes it through stricter isolation.
  • Yarn Berry can deliver strong reproducibility for large controlled environments, but Plug'n'Play still pays an ecosystem compatibility tax.
  • Bun and Deno change more than installation speed because each brings runtime assumptions into dependency management.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] How to read distributed traces when you didn’t write the code
item.description:
Gives a practical reading method for unfamiliar distributed traces: start at the root, classify spans, follow the critical path rather than the longest span, and use attributes and events to map runtime behavior back to code.

  • Treat a trace as production documentation: root span, service boundaries, child spans, attributes, and events describe the real request path.
  • The critical path is the chain of dependent operations that blocks completion; a long parallel span may contribute nothing to latency.
  • Common trace shapes point to failure modes: staircases suggest serial work or N+1 calls, gaps suggest uninstrumented work or waiting, and synchronized endings suggest timeouts or locks.
  • Span names, service names, code attributes, and exception events can narrow an unfamiliar code search to a repo, function, file, or line.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Inside the Miasma Software Supply Chain Attack Toolkit
item.description:
Dissects leaked Miasma source as a full supply-chain attack toolkit rather than a simple worm, spanning npm, PyPI, RubyGems, GitHub Actions, AI coding-tool config poisoning, SSH movement, secret harvesting, GitHub-based C2, and provenance abuse.

  • The toolkit uses GitHub itself as command, control, and exfiltration infrastructure, which weakens network-level C2 detection.
  • Stolen PATs can become infrastructure for later infections when exfiltration commits embed encrypted token payloads discoverable through GitHub search.
  • The npm path includes both stolen-token publishing and OIDC trusted-publishing abuse, including Sigstore provenance generation for trojanized packages.
  • AI coding-tool config files, GitHub Actions semver tags, deployment records, and runner memory are all treated as executable attack surfaces.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] How we sync Postgres to the browser: ElectricSQL for rows, Yjs for documents
item.description:
Explains Plain's realtime architecture as a substrate rather than a per-feature bolt-on: ElectricSQL mirrors authorized Postgres row subsets into browser stores, Yjs handles collaborative documents, and TanStack DB bridges optimistic local state with authoritative database updates.

  • Separate state by consistency model: authoritative rows belong to Postgres replication, while concurrent document editing belongs to CRDTs.
  • The Shape proxy is the tenancy boundary; clients choose allowed tables and org scope, while the server writes the actual filters.
  • Optimistic mutations are reconciled with authoritative Electric updates by matching transaction IDs, so overlays are confirmed or rolled back rather than left stale.
  • Making liveness the default pays off for developer platforms where issues, CI, messages, agents, and teammates mutate the same objects continuously.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] SQLite improving performance with pre-sort
item.description:
Shows that batching random primary-key inserts into SQLite can be improved by pre-sorting keys before insertion, because ordered B+ tree writes avoid page churn, splits, and rebalancing caused by random write order.

  • Random 160-bit keys preserve entropy but create the same insertion-locality problem as UUID4-style identifiers.
  • When records are already batched, sorting by key before insertion can make the storage engine's ordered data structure work with the workload rather than against it.
  • The optimization is workload-specific: it helps when random keys are inserted in large batches and the sort cost is lower than the B+ tree churn it prevents.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Optimization catalog. When float division beats integer division
item.description:
Walks through a counterintuitive hot-path optimization where replacing variable integer division with float64 division and conversion wins because IDIVQ throughput is worse than DIVSD for the measured independent divisions.

  • The benchmark showed a 28% Intel win and a smaller AMD win before broader unrolled variants were tested.
  • The speedup comes from replacing IDIVQ with DIVSD, not from removing divide-by-zero or overflow checks.
  • Correctness depends on numeric bounds: operands and quotient precision must stay within ranges where float64 represents the integer result exactly.
  • For repeated divisors, reciprocal multiplication or fast division libraries can beat the float trick; benchmark the actual hot path before changing code.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Making numpy-ts as fast as native
item.description:
Documents how numpy-ts moved from broad TypeScript NumPy compatibility at roughly 15x slower than native NumPy to parity by moving hot kernels to WASM and, more importantly, moving ndarray storage into WASM-owned memory to avoid copy-in/copy-out overhead.

  • Pure TypeScript scalar loops were not competing with Python; they were competing with NumPy's native SIMD, BLAS, LAPACK, and mature memory model.
  • WASM kernels cut the gap but hit a ceiling while every operation copied TypedArray data into and out of WASM linear memory.
  • The architectural turn was ownership of bytes: JavaScript kept metadata and ergonomics, while data buffers and kernel outputs stayed in WASM memory.
  • Native-like performance imports native-like problems, including allocator design, lifetime tracking, dtype gaps, and explicit cleanup for long-running workloads.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The biggest problem with AI is not correctness - it is architecture sanity
item.description:
Argues that the harder AI-code failure mode is architectural accumulation rather than local correctness: agents readily add schemas, converters, migrations, and tests, but rarely propose deleting state or simplifying a design boundary.

  • Tests and review can catch local defects while missing structural debt that makes future features harder to fit.
  • AI agents tend to extend existing shape instead of challenging whether the shape should exist.
  • Manual architecture and schema review remains the controlling layer; agents are better used to fill implementation space inside boundaries that humans set.
  • The failure appears later, when a major feature no longer fits the accumulated schema and the agent starts producing plausible but misaligned code.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Mentoring juniors is still alive in the age of AI
item.description:
Frames junior mentoring in the AI era around system-design mental models rather than code generation: durable data contracts, composability, editable state, brittleness, failure modes, extensibility, scale, and separation still determine whether AI-assisted implementation works.

  • The teachable skill is not typing code faster; it is decomposing products into schemas, queues, artifacts, contracts, and state transitions.
  • AI can help juniors ask theory questions, but it does not replace the need to reason about durability, failure modes, and boundaries.
  • Juniors who vibe-code without architecture return with brittle systems; juniors who model the system first can use AI to fill in implementation effectively.

item.title: [Psychology] AI construed as a brilliant friend...Claude's New Constitution
item.description:
Critiques Anthropic's new Claude constitution as a revealing artifact: a private firm tries to encode safety, usefulness, personality, discretion, and commercial viability into a model while speaking to it in language normally reserved for moral agents.

  • The hierarchy of safety, ethics, policy compliance, and helpfulness is read as a business model written in moral language.
  • Anthropomorphic training can become circular: models are taught the language of selfhood, then that language is treated as evidence of inner life.
  • Preference for standards over rules relocates discretion into the model and the company's priors rather than eliminating power.
  • The essay's strongest warning is over-reliance on a warm expert-like tool that lacks licensing, fiduciary duties, and ordinary accountability.

item.title: [Visualization] Bot traffic surpasses human traffic
item.description:
Notes Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince's claim that agentic traffic has pushed bot traffic past human traffic online, only nine months after bots were around 30% of web traffic.

  • AI scraping and agentic traffic are changing the baseline assumption that web traffic primarily represents human attention.
  • Analytics, rate limits, caching, content economics, and abuse detection become harder when automated traffic is the majority.
  • The item is short, but the direction of travel is a useful marker for product, publishing, and infrastructure planning.
